figures.caption: Figure 5: A toy example illustrating the impact of token pruning on spatial reasoning. Although the rectangle itself remains visually unchanged, removing background tokens in a spatially biased manner distorts the spatial distribution of the retained tokens and leads to incorrect localization. In contrast, spatially uniform pruning preserves layout consistency and maintains correct predictions.

Table 4: Comparison of token pruning methods on the AndroidControl dataset. Type denotes action type accuracy, Text denotes textual type accuracy, Ground denotes grounding accuracy, and Step denotes step success accuracy. Upper Bound refers to the model without token pruning, where all visual tokens are preserved.

Figure 2: Overview of our study on token pruning for historical screenshots in GUI visual agents. We highlight three key perspectives for effective pruning: semantic balance between foreground and background, spatially consistent token retention, and allocating more tokens to recent screenshots inspired by the recency effect.

Table 5: Effect of time-decay token allocation on different pruning methods. "-TD" denotes our time-decay variant. "ratio" indicates the performance retention compared to its corresponding baseline method.

Figure 6: Left: In the conventional implementation, pruned tokens are directly removed, while the remaining tokens retain their original position indices with unchanged positional encodings. Right: We argue that corresponding compensation for the positional encodings of the remaining tokens is required after token pruning.

Figure 3: Illustration of the edge-based foreground–background partition on a GUI screenshot. The upper panel shows the original screenshot, while the lower panel presents the patch-wise labeling based on edge responses. Patches without significant edge information are marked with red crosses and identified as background.
